query: As of early July 2026, what percentage of Russia's national oil-refining capacity is offline/disabled due to Ukrainian drone strikes?
answer:
I've completed the research. Here's a summary of my findings on Russia's oil-refining capacity offline due to Ukrainian drone strikes as of early July 2026:

## Key Findings

**The headline estimates cluster around 20–43%**, but they measure different things:

| Source | Figure | Notes |
|--------|--------|-------|
| Ukrainian General Staff | **42.7% (~43%)** | Official claim from a conflict party; $13.5B losses since Aug 2025 |
| Financial Times (analyst estimates) | **20–40%** | July 8–9, 2026 |
| Independent energy analysts (Fortune) | **25%+** | July 4, 2026 |

**Critical nuance — "capacity offline" ≠ "output lost":**
- Russia processed ~4.1 million bbl/day in June 2026 — **35% below nominal capacity** and 28% below the five-year average (FT)
- Fuel production fell **~25% year-on-year** in June, running ~20% below domestic demand (Reuters)
- Russia's large spare-capacity cushion (~20% habitually idle) means physical damage translates into a smaller sustained output loss than the headline figures suggest

**Best neutral answer:** roughly **25–40% of capacity disabled/offline** at the July 2026 peak, with the ~43% figure as an upper bound from Ukraine and sub-20% figures reflecting spare-capacity-adjusted throughput measures.

**Context:** Ukraine escalated sharply from May 2026, hitting 10+ major refineries including Omsk (Russia's largest, ~7% of capacity, struck July 6–7). The crisis triggered fuel restrictions across ~50 regions affecting ~50 million people (~35% of the population), a Crimea state of emergency, quality-standard downgrades (Euro-5→Euro-3), and fuel imports from India, Kazakhstan, and Belarus.

I've cross-validated across seven independent sources (Kyiv Post, FT via Pravda/UNN, Fortune, Wikipedia, Lviv Herald analysis, Militarnyi, Moscow Times) and delivered the full structured output. Note this is a fast-moving situation where figures shift with each wave of strikes and repairs.

sources.title: Ukrainian Defense Forces Disable 43% of Russia's Total Oil Refining Capacity
sources.snippet: The General Staff of the Armed Forces of Ukraine announced that ongoing strikes have disabled 42.7% of Russia's total oil refining capacity, inflicting an estimated $13.5 billion in industry losses since August 2025.

sources.title: Ukrainian drones knock out up to 40% of Russia's oil refining capacity – FT
sources.snippet: Ukrainian drone strikes may have knocked out between 20% and 40% of Russia's oil refining capacity. In June, Russia was processing an average of 4.1 million barrels of oil per day, 28% below the five-year average and 35% below nominal capacity.

sources.title: Ukrainian drone strikes have disabled up to 40% of Russia's oil refining capacity - Financial Times
sources.snippet: According to analysts' estimates, in June Russia processed 4.1 million barrels of oil per day, which is 28% less than the average.

sources.title: Russia's fuel crisis is so bad that a mom and her baby waited in line for 18 hours to get gas
sources.snippet: Energy analysts have estimated that 25% or more of Russia's refining capacity has been taken offline by the attacks.

sources.title: 2025–2026 Russian fuel crisis - Wikipedia
sources.snippet: By July 2026, most of Russia's regions were experiencing fuel restrictions, directly affecting 50 million people, approximately 35% of the Russian population.

sources.title: Loss of Russian refinery capacity
sources.snippet: Nameplate capacity physically hit or periodically disrupted: perhaps 30–40% at some point. Capacity actually offline at a given peak: typically 15–25%. Net loss of effective throughput: roughly 3–6% so far.

sources.title: Omsk Oil Refinery Lost 75% of Its Production Capacity and Suspended Operations
sources.snippet: Russia's largest oil refinery has suspended operations after losing a significant portion of its production capacity following an attack by Ukrainian drones.

sources.title: Russia's Largest Oil Refinery Halts Production After Drone Attack, Sources Say
sources.snippet: Russia's largest oil refinery in the Omsk region of western Siberia has halted operations following a Ukrainian drone attack.

key_facts: Ukrainian Armed Forces General Staff claimed strikes disabled 42.7% (~43%) of Russia's total projected oil refining capacity as of early July 2026, with $13.5 billion in industry losses since August 2025.
Financial Times (July 8–9, 2026), citing analyst estimates, reported Ukrainian drone strikes may have knocked out between 20% and 40% of Russia's oil refining capacity.
Independent energy analysts estimated 25% or more of Russia's refining capacity had been taken offline (Fortune, July 4, 2026).
In June 2026 Russia processed an average of 4.1 million barrels/day — 28% below the five-year average and 35% below nominal capacity (FT).
Reuters estimated Russia's overall fuel production fell ~25% year-on-year in June 2026 and was running ~20% below domestic demand.
Russia's total nominal primary refining capacity is ~6.5–6.6 million barrels/day (~320–330 million tonnes/year).

Ukraine sharply intensified strikes from May 2026; since then 10 of Russia's largest refineries were attacked, including the Omsk refinery (~7% of national capacity, ~2,500 km from front), which halted operations after a July 6–7 strike.
Fuel restrictions affected ~50 million Russians (~35% of population) across ~50 regions by late June 2026; Crimea declared a state of emergency with petrol sold via electronic vouchers.

A key analytical distinction: nameplate capacity 'hit' (~30–40%) versus capacity actually offline at peak (~15–25%) versus net sustained throughput loss after spare-capacity substitution (historically ~3–6%, widening in 2026).

Russia mitigated by lowering fuel quality standards (Euro-5 to Euro-3), banning gasoline exports, and importing fuel from India (60,000+ tonnes), Kazakhstan (50,000 tonnes), and Belarus (100,000–150,000 tonnes/month).
The 2025–2026 fuel crisis began in August 2025 with escalating drone attacks, escalating dramatically in mid-2026.
